title: Pork Chops with Cider and Apples
subtitle: Pan-fried pork chops finished in dry cider with caramelised apple and a cream pan sauce
excerpt: Pork chops with cider and apples is an autumn dish built around three things that belong together. The pork is browned hard, the apple slices are cooked in the same fat, and the cider and cream make a quick pan sauce from the residue. The whole dish is done in under 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: British and Norman French tradition. The pork and apple combination is documented across British and French cooking from the medieval period; dry cider as a cooking medium for pork is particularly associated with the apple-growing regions of south-west England and Normandy.

recipe.temperatureNote: Hob only; no oven temperature required.

glossaryTerms.definition: To add liquid to a hot pan after frying in order to loosen the caramelised deposits from the base and dissolve them into a sauce. Those deposits are concentrated flavour; this step is how a simple pan sauce develops depth and colour.

content.text: Pork and apple is a pairing so natural that it requires almost no technique to make it work. Brown the chops, colour the apples, build a sauce from the pan residue and the cider, add cream. The sequence matters: everything happens in the same pan, in the right order. The pan residue is the flavour.

items.prepNote: eating apple, cored and cut into thick wedges

content.text: Season the {{pork-chop}} pork chops generously on both sides. Heat a little oil in a wide frying pan over a high heat until it shimmers. Add the chops and brown for 3 to 4 minutes per side until well coloured. Remove from the pan and set aside on a plate.
Add the {{apple}} apple wedges to the same pan and cook over a medium-high heat for 2 to 3 minutes per side until golden. Remove and set aside with the pork.
Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{shallot}} shallots. Cook for 2 minutes until softened. Add the thyme. Pour in the {{dry-cider}} of dry cider and
the pan, scraping up all the residue. Let the cider bubble for 2 minutes to reduce slightly.

Stir in the {{double-cream}} of double cream and the mustard. Return the pork chops to the pan. Simmer gently for 5 minutes, turning the chops once, until they are cooked through. Add the apple wedges back for the final minute to warm through. Season the sauce and rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.cause: Cooked for too long in the sauce after an already vigorous sear.
items.fix: The pork only needs 5 minutes in the sauce after browning. Remove the pan from the heat as soon as there is no pink at the thickest point. Pork chops continue to cook in the residual heat.

items.cause: Cooked too long, or a very soft eating apple variety.
items.fix: Cook the apple wedges briefly, just enough to colour them. Add them back into the sauce for the final minute only. Cox or Braeburn apples hold their shape better than Golden Delicious.

items.cause: Cider was too acidic and did not reduce enough.
items.fix: Let the cider bubble hard for 3 to 4 minutes before adding the cream to drive off the acidity. The cream will temper the sharpness further.

content.text: Pork and apple is one of the oldest pairings in British cooking, documented from the medieval period through to the Victorian era. Cider as a cooking medium for pork is most associated with Devon, Somerset, and the other apple-growing counties of south-west England, where both ingredients came from the same landscape. Dry cider is essential; sweet cider makes the sauce cloying.
